tracks.name: Kohola
tracks.story: Tagged in the Au'Au Channel off Maui in winter breeding grounds. Migrates ~5,000km north each year to feed on krill in the cold productive waters of southeast Alaska.

tracks.name: Sirocco
tracks.story: Fledged on South Georgia Island. Circumnavigates the Southern Ocean on the roaring forties wind belt — covering over 120,000km in a single year without ever returning to land.

tracks.name: Mary Lee
tracks.story: Tagged off Cape Cod by OCEARCH. Travels thousands of kilometers each year along the US Atlantic coast — pinging from Florida shorelines to Nova Scotia in a single season.

tracks.name: Polaris
tracks.story: The longest migration on Earth. Breeds on the cliffs of Greenland in summer, then flies pole-to-pole down the Atlantic to feed in Antarctic waters during the southern summer — ~70,000km annually.

tracks.name: Plymouth
tracks.story: An irruptive migrant — most years stays high in the Canadian Arctic, but in winters of low lemming numbers pushes far south. This bird flew from Nunavut to a runway near Boston.

tracks.name: Lonesome Don
tracks.story: Tracked across the Alcedo volcano on Isabela Island. Moves up the slopes during the wet season to graze, then descends back to the lowlands when grasses dry — a vertical migration of just 10km that takes months.
